channel.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive
channel.description: A daily analysis of all of the AI and global news of the day I find from subscribing to a bunch of interesting newsletters
channel.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
channel.itunes:summary: A daily analysis of all of the AI and global news of the day I find from subscribing to a bunch of interesting newsletters

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - July 1, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information on why GPU server prices are becoming unpredictable as soaring demand for memory chips pushes up costs for Nvidia AI servers, making it harder for cloud providers to budget and forcing them to raise prices for AI developer customers; The Information exclusive on Anthropic preparing to deploy a Claude agent inside Microsoft Teams even as Salesforce employees worry about Anthropic's invasion of Slack — showing how Anthropic is simultaneously partnering with and threatening enterprise software giants; The Information on AI chip startup Ayar Labs joining the chip funding spree with optical networking technology that could transform data center interconnects; The Information AI Agenda on OpenAI engineers discovering a way to more than halve the cost of inference through newly-discovered optimizations, reducing GPU needs for ChatGPT to just a few hundred Nvidia chips at one point; The Information exclusive on AI video startup Higgsfield AI in talks to raise at a $5 billion valuation — more than quadrupling its January valuation — with $500 million in revenue run rate and 70% enterprise customers; Ignite Insights' deep dive into whether next-token prediction is all you need for superintelligence, covering Anthropic's Fable 5 launch and disappearance, the limits of chain-of-thought reasoning, and the philosophical debate over whether scaling alone gets us to AGI; Ethan Mollick on the twilight of the chatbot era as AI work shifts from chat interfaces to autonomous agents that can complete multi-step tasks independently; and Scott Alexander's Astral Codex Ten with a charming personal essay on parenthood and genetics — how his children have inherited his train obsession, OCD patterns, and even his writing instincts, with a meditation on the 'feudal code' of toddler negotiation.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup: The Information on GPU server price unpredictability from AI memory chip demand; Anthropic's Claude agent launching in Microsoft Teams while Salesforce employees worry about Anthropic invading Slack; Ayar Labs joining the AI chip funding spree; OpenAI engineers halving inference costs with new compute optimizations; Higgsfield AI video startup raising at $5B valuation with $500M ARR; Ignite Insights on whether next-token prediction is all you need for superintelligence; Ethan Mollick on the twilight of chatbots as AI shifts to autonomous agents; and Scott Alexander on parenthood, genetics, and the feudal code of toddler negotiations.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 30, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: Oncology CoPilot on Marlo — a pediatric oncology precision medicine platform connecting patients with personalized treatment options and clinical trials, built by cancer survivors and advocates who experienced firsthand the gap between diagnosis and finding the right specialized care; PitchBook on how liquidity is loosening in Asia-Pacific private markets with APAC VC deals picking up across sectors including AI infrastructure and fintech, and LPs starting to receive long-awaited distributions as the regional private capital cycle turns; PitchBook on the billions flowing into AI video as Google DeepMind's $75 million investment in A24 signals a shift from pure model-building to AI-native studios and interactive content infrastructure — with $5.6 billion invested in AI video in 2026 already up 43% from 2025; Oussama Ammar's philosophical essay Reveiller le Golem on Norbert Wiener — the founder of cybernetics who warned in 1950 that machines cannot be given instructions abstract enough to avoid catastrophic outcomes, drawing the parallel between Wiener's Golem of Prague allegory and today's AI alignment challenges; and Exponential View's weekly data roundup showing AI quarterly revenues now exceeding AI capex depreciation expense, China's AI labs hiring talent with just 1.6 years of experience versus 5.5 years in the US, SK Hynix overtaking Samsung Electronics in market value for the first time, Morgan Stanley tripling its humanoid robot forecast to 50,000 Chinese shipments this year, and roughly half of workers believing AI can already do half or more of their work tasks.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup: Oncology CoPilot on Marlo — precision medicine for pediatric oncology connecting patients with clinical trials; PitchBook on APAC private capital liquidity loosening with VC deals picking up; PitchBook on billions flowing into AI video with Google DeepMind's $75M A24 bet and $5.6B invested YTD up 43%; Oussama Ammar's philosophical essay on Norbert Wiener and AI alignment — the Golem allegory for why you cannot specify instructions abstractly enough; and Exponential View data: AI revenues now exceed capex depreciation, China hires AI talent at 1.6 years experience vs. 5.5 in US, SK Hynix overtakes Samsung, and half of workers think AI can do half their job.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 29, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information exclusive on Baidu's AI chip unit Kunlunxin planning a $50 billion Hong Kong IPO while requiring investors to commit to buying chips worth 3-7x their subscription amount -- a novel arrangement reflecting intense competition in China's crowded domestic AI chip market as the company seeks SMIC manufacturing after missing China's secure chip certification list; The Information Sunday Insights on how five Chinese firms now rank among the world's 10 biggest ad sellers as retail media spending surges to $175.7 billion globally and traditional media companies disappear from the top rankings -- with China as the largest retail media market at $77.6 billion and ByteDance, Tencent, Alibaba, PDD, JD.com, and Kuaishou all making the list; and PitchBook on how AI video investment is evolving from model funding to studios and interactive infrastructure -- with $5.6 billion invested in 2026 already up 43% from 2025, Google DeepMind's $75 million A24 investment signaling a shift beyond pure video-generation tools, and AI-native studios like Wonder and Promise raising VC to build IP catalogs as the new content plays.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup: Baidu's Kunlunxin targets $50B Hong Kong IPO while demanding investors buy chips worth 3-7x their investment -- competing in China's crowded domestic AI chip market; five Chinese firms including ByteDance, Tencent, Alibaba, PDD, and JD.com now rank in the world's 10 biggest ad sellers as retail media hits $175.7B globally; and PitchBook on AI video investment evolving from models to studios with Google DeepMind's A24 bet and $5.6B invested in 2026 already up 43% from all of 2025.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 28, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information exclusive on Salesforce employees' concerns over Anthropic's Claude Tag invading Slack -- how Anthropic launched a competing AI teammate directly inside Slack while Salesforce CEO Benioff calls it a partner, not a competitor; The Information Weekend on where tech political spending goes next after AI-affiliated groups spent $30 million in the New York Congressional primary won by Micah Lasher over AI regulation author Alex Bores by 5,000 votes; Passing the Torch Newsletter on the AI Memory Tax -- how DRAM contract prices jumped 98% in Q1 2026 as Micron signs $100 billion in take-or-pay agreements and SK Hynix sells 90% of its premier AI memory to a single customer, transferring costs to consumers via $300 Apple laptop price hikes; Azeem Azhar's Exponential View on fifty years of Moore's Law not being fast enough for AI -- tracking the break in the 50-year compute growth trend driven by AI accelerators with implications for the next decade; The Batch from DeepLearning.AI on a new generation studying AI, Apple's recipe for on-device models, and GLM5.2 tackling open-ended problems; PitchBook on where the LBOs aren't as private credit lenders shut the door on software buyouts; and PitchBook Research on how SpaceX prices the AI race.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup: Anthropic's Claude Tag invades Slack creating tensions inside Salesforce; tech political spending heats up after $30M NY Congressional primary battle; the AI Memory Tax with DRAM prices up 98% in Q1 2026 and $300 Apple laptop surcharges; Exponential View on breaking the 50-year Moore's Law compute trend; DeepLearning.AI Batch on AI education; PitchBook on LBO drought and SpaceX pricing the AI race.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 27, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information's Big Read on Robin Zeng and CATL — how China's battery giant, which controls 40% of the world's lithium-ion battery supply, became indispensable to AI data centers, EVs, and the global energy transition, with $10.5 billion in 2024 profits and a $56 billion founder net worth; The Information exclusive on how Anthropic's Mythos model spooked DeepSeek, prompting CEO Liang Wenfeng to complete a record $7.4 billion first-time fundraising at a $50+ billion valuation and plan to at least double its 300-person workforce; The Information AM on Apple raising Mac and iPad prices due to an 'extraordinary' surge in memory and storage component costs driven by AI data center demand, with the MacBook Neo jumping from $599 to $699 and MacBook Pro from $1,699 to $1,999; PitchBook's Quantum Leap on quantum computing's record investment run; and The Batch from DeepLearning.AI on the latest in AI research and applications.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup: Robin Zeng and CATL — the Chinese battery giant controlling 40% of global lithium-ion supply with $10.5B in 2024 profits, powering AI data centers and EVs; Anthropic's Mythos spooked DeepSeek into completing a record $7.4B first fundraising at $50B+ valuation, planning to double its 300-person team; Apple raises Mac and iPad prices due to extraordinary AI-driven memory component shortage; PitchBook on quantum computing's record investment run; and DeepLearning.AI's The Batch.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 26, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information on the Trump administration asking OpenAI to stagger the release of its new GPT-5.6 model over national security concerns, highlighting growing industry confusion around government review processes for new AI models; The Information on Google striking a tough negotiating stance with publishers on AI licensing, pitching a pilot program that ties content licensing to AI promotion features while offering below-market rates; The Information AI Agenda on the rift between World Models and Vision-Language-Action models dividing the physical AI field as robotics companies bet on fundamentally different approaches to machine intelligence; The Information Applied AI on Anthropic customers discovering errant billing charges that overstated their actual compute usage, as an AI auditing startup uncovers systematic discrepancies; The Information Dealmaker on Citi pitching a cleanup of the murky Special Purpose Vehicle market; The Information on Google revamping its AI coding strike team amid struggles to catch up with Anthropic; The Information AM covering OpenAI and Broadcom unveiling a new AI server chip; PitchBook on the Whoop-ification of the World Cup and how wearable health technology is transforming elite sports monitoring; Astral Codex Ten on the Metaculus Democracy Threat Index, a crowdsourced prediction-market-style tool for measuring threats to American democracy that currently shows declining concern since November 2025; Gary Marcus on the Generative AI Fizzle, tracking declining AI stock valuations and questioning whether the AI bubble is deflating slowly rather than popping; and Exponential View's first bottom-up measure of the AI economy showing $110 billion in deduplicated revenue over the past 12 months with a $175 billion annualized run rate.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup: Trump administration asks OpenAI to stagger GPT-5.6 release over security concerns; Google strikes tough AI licensing stance with publishers; World Models vs. VLAs dividing physical AI and robotics; Anthropic customers discover errant billing overcharges; Citi pitches SPV market cleanup; Google revamps AI coding strike team to chase Anthropic; OpenAI and Broadcom unveil new AI server chip; PitchBook on Whoop tech at the World Cup; Metaculus Democracy Threat Index showing declining threat levels since November 2025; Gary Marcus on the Generative AI Fizzle and deflating AI stocks; and Exponential View's $110B bottom-up measure of the AI economy.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 25, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information on Intel-backed AI chip firm SambaNova quintupling its valuation to $10 billion following an $800 million-plus funding round; The Information on 300-plus local bans and moratoriums threatening the U.S. data center boom as communities push back against power consumption and noise; xAI betting on Grok's racy side as Elon Musk's AI lab leans into adult content generation to monetize its user base; Kalshi passing $2 billion in annualized revenue and holding early IPO talks; Microsoft tightening its grip on GPU allocations and pressuring AI customers to commit to longer-term cloud contracts; The Information AM covering OpenAI executives touting ad business progress at Cannes Lions with 20% of ChatGPT queries showing direct commercial intent, Meta unveiling new smart glasses at $299 targeting mass market adoption ahead of Apple and Google rivals, and Alibaba suing the Pentagon over a government contract; PitchBook on the PE midyear outlook, record robotics VC investment, European defense tech investment surge, and the global data center market; and the Sinica Podcast on agile governance as Tsinghua's Xue Lan explains how China regulates transformative technologies without stifling innovation, recorded at the World Economic Forum in Dalian.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup: Intel-backed SambaNova quintuples valuation to $10B; 300-plus data center bans threatening the U.S. AI infrastructure boom; xAI monetizing Grok with adult content; Kalshi at $2B annualized revenue in early IPO talks; Microsoft squeezing AI customers on GPU allocations; OpenAI touts 20% commercial-intent ChatGPT queries at Cannes; Meta launches $299 smart glasses; Alibaba sues the Pentagon; PitchBook on PE midyear, record robotics VC, and European defense tech; and Sinica Podcast on China's agile governance approach to regulating AI.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 24, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information on XLight, a U.S.-backed chipmaking startup chaired by former Intel CEO Pat Gelsinger, raising $350 million amid the domestic semiconductor push; The Information AM covering how AI customers including major enterprises are lowering their Anthropic and OpenAI bills by using smaller, cheaper models and negotiating volume discounts as AI spending faces ROI scrutiny; The Information AI Agenda on agent loops and how the shift from single-step AI to multi-step agentic workflows is transforming enterprise software deployment; The Information Applied AI on Together AI and Hugging Face competing to serve the open source AI market as Together AI doubles its revenue and Hugging Face expands its enterprise offerings; The Information Dealmaker on Walmart acquiring Vibe, a connected TV advertising platform, to bolster its retail media network and compete with Amazon's advertising business; and PitchBook on the venture capital market outlook, emerging manager fundraising dynamics, and the latest VC deal flow and exit trends in the current market environment.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup: XLight raises $350M for U.S. domestic chipmaking under former Intel CEO Pat Gelsinger; enterprises cutting Anthropic and OpenAI bills with smaller models and volume deals; agent loops transforming enterprise software; Together AI and Hugging Face battle for the open source AI market; Walmart acquires connected TV ad platform Vibe to compete with Amazon retail media; and PitchBook on VC market dynamics and deal flow.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 23, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information on ex-DOGE employees Brooks Morgan and Adam Ramada raising $225 million for Banner VC while co-leading a $75 million investment in orbital infrastructure startup Impulse Space; The Information AM covering Uber named as anchor investor for Lime's IPO at a $1.8 billion valuation with Uber owning 10%+ of Lime and generating 14% of its revenue, Nobel laureate John Jumper departing Google DeepMind for Anthropic, Amazon MGM Studios dropping its nearly-complete Sam Altman movie, Sen. Bernie Sanders introducing a $7 trillion AI sovereign wealth fund bill for 50% public ownership of top AI companies, Manus revenue soaring to $400M-$500M annualized as original Chinese investors move to reverse the Meta deal, Penske Media acquiring Vox Media titles including The Verge and SB Nation, and Accenture stock falling 18% to a 9-year low on declining bookings; The Information AI Agenda on why Canva sees ChatGPT and Claude as complementary distribution channels rather than competitors — adding millions of new users via chatbot integrations — plus John Jumper and Noam Shazeer joining Anthropic and OpenAI respectively in a deepening AI talent drain from Google; The Information AI Infrastructure on data centers transforming rural American communities — from $50,000 teacher bonuses in Louisiana to 5,970 permanent jobs in Wisconsin — and why communities have more leverage than they realize to negotiate benefits from tech developers; The Information Electric on Lithium Americas and its Thacker Pass Nevada mine containing the largest known U.S. lithium deposit, with GM claiming its entire 20-year output and the U.S. government taking a 5% equity stake; The Information on OpenAI's unusual IPO balance sheet showing zero debt and just $46M in quarterly capex despite $665 billion in off-balance-sheet chip and data center commitments; and Astral Codex Ten on the medical debate over whole-body screening — whether the benefit of catching cancers early outweighs the harm of false positives, anxiety, and unnecessary procedures.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup: Ex-DOGE staffers raise $225M for Banner VC; Uber anchors Lime's IPO at $1.8B valuation; Nobel laureate John Jumper joins Anthropic from DeepMind; Amazon MGM drops Sam Altman movie; Bernie Sanders introduces $7T AI sovereign wealth fund bill; Manus revenue surges to $400M-$500M ARR as China reverses Meta deal; Accenture stock falls 18%; Canva sees AI chatbots as distribution partners not competitors; data centers bringing $50K teacher bonuses and 5,970 Wisconsin jobs; Lithium Americas' Thacker Pass mine with GM as sole buyer; OpenAI's unusual IPO balance sheet hides $665B in commitments; and Astral Codex Ten on the whole-body screening medical debate.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 22, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information on Lime's IPO — Uber named as anchor investor raising $200M at a $1.8B valuation with Uber's >10% stake and $85M invested in 2020, guaranteeing Lime's loan and driving 14% of its revenue; The Information on Morgan Stanley pitching data center developers to tap the $1.4T leveraged loan market — the CoreWeave $3.1B CLO deal drew $19B in orders, with JPMorgan projecting $350B across bonds and loans over five years; The Information on SpaceX's 74-day IPO setting pace for OpenAI and Anthropic — the Trump SEC's hands-off approach shaved 37% off the typical IPO timeline, with mid-August listings now possible for both companies; Passing the Torch Newsletter on the Thirty-Minute Ceasefire — analyzing the Islamabad MOU between the US and Iran, why Hezbollah's firing on Israel ended it within hours, and the classic geopolitical whodunit of who started it; Sinica Podcast's Kaiser Kuo on how China built the energy system the world now needs — using the Iran war's Strait of Hormuz disruption to show how China's EV fleet alone displaces 1 million barrels of oil per day and how structural electrification created real energy resilience; and Words That Matter's guest curator Afra Wang on AI and human expression, Ken Liu's view of technology as fundamentally humanistic, the question of AI consciousness, and whether machines can truly create.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup: Lime IPO names Uber as anchor investor at $1.8B valuation; Morgan Stanley pitches data centers on the $1.4T leveraged loan market with CoreWeave CLO drawing $19B in orders; SpaceX's 74-day IPO sets pace for OpenAI and Anthropic mid-August listings; the Thirty-Minute Ceasefire — why the Islamabad MOU broke down within hours; Sinica Podcast on how China's EV-driven energy transformation created real resilience when the Strait of Hormuz closed; and Afra Wang on AI consciousness, human expression, and Ken Liu's humanistic view of technology.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 21, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: PitchBook on how private equity firms are racing to embed AI into portfolio companies but struggling to show financial results — 73% of executives expect AI to increase portfolio value over the next 12 months but only 8% report material EBITDA impact, while most AI adoption remains focused on back-office efficiency rather than revenue growth; private credit lenders shutting the door on software buyouts leaving PE sponsors with signed due diligence and no funding; Blackstone, Apollo Global Management, and FS KKR taking over software company Medallia from Thoma Bravo; SpaceX's $60 billion acquisition of AI coding startup Cursor; Azeem Azhar's Exponential View #579 on how AI-native startups are 25% smaller with more engineers and fewer managers than peers, the critical importance of rebuilding products around AI closed loops rather than just giving workers AI tools, Sam Altman on how AI has transformed what a startup can accomplish, and the Bitter Lesson — generalist frontier AI models outperforming best-in-class specialist medical tools in head-to-head tests with two-thirds of US physicians now using OpenEvidence; GenAI driving nearly 2% of traffic to Walmart and Target; and the Sinica Podcast on the Tianjin Massacre of June 21, 1870 — when anti-Christian riots led to the killing of French diplomats, nuns, and Chinese converts, bringing China and France to the brink of war and revealing deep fault lines between Chinese society and Western colonial presence.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup: PitchBook on PE firms failing to turn AI into revenue growth — 73% expect AI gains but only 8% report material EBITDA impact; private credit shutting the door on software buyouts; Blackstone, Apollo, and FS KKR taking Medallia from Thoma Bravo; SpaceX's $60B Cursor acquisition; Exponential View #579 on AI-native startups being 25% smaller, rebuilding products around AI closed loops, Sam Altman on startup transformation, and generalist AI beating specialist medical models; GenAI driving 2% of Walmart & Target traffic; and Sinica Podcast on the Tianjin Massacre of 1870 — when anti-Christian riots brought China and France to the brink of war.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 20, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: NFX on AI's New Industrial Revolution and the existential power infrastructure limiter; SpaceX's $60 billion acquisition of Cursor — the largest VC-backed startup acquisition in history; Google and Microsoft unveiling the ARD standard to challenge Anthropic's MCP in enterprise AI; Cursor launching Origin to compete with GitHub; Kalshi passing $2 billion in annualized revenue with early IPO talks; Manus' revenue soaring to $400M-$500M ARR as Chinese investors move to reverse the Meta deal under government order; Apple raising prices amid memory chip shortages; Hermes from Nous Research emerging as a competitor to OpenClaw with self-teaching capabilities; clean energy PE reaching a record $63.8 billion in Q1 2026; and middle market PE trends with sponsor-to-sponsor deals dominating exits at 69.5% of exit value.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 19, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: SpaceX's $60 billion acquisition of Cursor spurring hopes for more mega VC-backed deals as M&A markets heat up; Kalshi passes $2 billion in annualized revenue and enters early IPO talks; Google and Microsoft teaming up to beat back Anthropic and OpenAI in the enterprise AI market; Amazon giving big brands more control over product listings; NFX on AI as the New Industrial Revolution and the existential limiter facing the current wave of AI; a competitor to OpenClaw emerges in the AI coding tools space; Manus' revenue soars as original investors move to reverse their Meta deal; Apple preparing to raise prices amid memory and storage shortages; Scott Alexander's Astral Codex Ten with preliminary thoughts on the Midjourney Scanner; Gary Marcus on Accenture then and now as a signifier of broader enterprise AI trends; and Auren Hoffman on disposable software — how software is becoming paper plates in the AI age.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup: SpaceX's $60B Cursor acquisition spurs M&A hopes; Kalshi at $2B annualized revenue in early IPO talks; Google and Microsoft team up against Anthropic and OpenAI; Amazon brand control over listings; NFX on AI as the New Industrial Revolution; a competitor to OpenClaw emerges; Manus' revenue surges as investors try to reverse Meta deal; Apple price hikes amid memory shortages; Scott Alexander on the Midjourney Scanner; Gary Marcus on Accenture as an AI bellwether; and Auren Hoffman on software becoming paper plates.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 18, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: Noam Shazeer — the legendary Google AI researcher who created the transformer architecture improvements and co-authored "Attention Is All You Need" — joining OpenAI after years at Character.ai, in what sources call the biggest individual AI talent acquisition ever; SpaceX finalizing a $60 billion deal to acquire Cursor (Anysphere), the AI coding assistant, creating the largest VC-backed acquisition in history and adding it to SpaceX's growing AI tools portfolio alongside xAI; OpenAI and Anthropic aggressively recruiting from Salesforce, poaching Salesforce AI and engineering talent as the enterprise AI hiring wars heat up; Amazon CEO Andy Jassy raising concerns with Anthropic about its content moderation policies and AI safety restrictions, which are creating friction with Amazon's AWS customers; Anthropic blindsiding business partners like Figma and Canva by building competing tools directly after deploying Claude in their products; OpenAI burning $3.7 billion in losses in Q1 2026 on $5.7 billion in revenue, showing the massive scale of capital required to compete in frontier AI; Temasek's deepening AI investment push across infrastructure and model companies; Azeem Azhar's Exponential View examining whether AI development is immune to groupthink and the risks of consensus thinking in a fast-moving field; What's Really Behind So-Called AI Layoffs from Gary Marcus's newsletter; and Scott Alexander's Astral Codex Ten essay "Waiting For The Miracle" on long-term AI expectations.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup: Noam Shazeer joins OpenAI from Character.ai in the biggest individual AI talent acquisition ever; SpaceX acquires Cursor for $60 billion in the largest VC-backed M&A deal in history; OpenAI and Anthropic poach Salesforce talent; Amazon CEO Jassy raises moderation concerns with Anthropic; Anthropic blindsides business partners by competing with its own API customers; OpenAI burns $3.7 billion in Q1 2026 losses; Temasek's AI push; Exponential View on AI groupthink risk; and Scott Alexander's "Waiting For The Miracle."

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 17, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: OpenAI burned $3.7 billion in Q1 2026 with $5.7B in revenue but $3.7B in losses; Anthropic banning staff from helping foreign governments build AI amid escalating export control scrutiny from the White House; DeepSeek closing its first-ever funding round raising over $7.4 billion with an unusual structure giving CEO Liang Wenfeng absolute control; enterprise AI cost management strategies and token-minimizing approaches; cybersecurity VC investments tracking; and prediction market growth at Polymarket and Kalshi.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 16, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: DeepSeek closes its first funding round raising over $7.4 billion with an unusual limited partnership structure that lets CEO Liang Wenfeng retain absolute control, with a five-year investor lockup; Qualcomm in talks to acquire Tenstorrent, the AI chip startup backed by Jeff Hinton and Hyundai, to expand its AI capabilities; Inside Broadcom's bold move to offer financing to hyperscalers for custom AI chip projects; Nvidia's share of the AI inference market appears to be rising despite new competitors; Chinese EV makers including BYD profiting from increased demand tied to the Iran war despite US sanctions pressure; AI's cost problem becomes front and center in Asia as enterprises weigh ROI; The Batch from DeepLearning.AI on Cursor's Composer 2.5, agents building agents, and myth-inspired AI storytelling; Oussama Ammar on why AI interfaces need warmth and kawaii aesthetics; Azeem Azhar's Exponential View data on AI productivity metrics; and the Sinica Podcast's review of Korean Messiah, Jonathan Cheng's book on Jim Jones and South Korean evangelism.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 15, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: OpenAI and Anthropic employees cashing out a combined $14 billion in shares over the last five years — OpenAI facilitated over $9 billion via tender offers while both companies still need massive public capital for model training; the longevity startup paradox where NewLimit ($630M+ raised), Retro Biosciences ($1.8B valuation), and Life Biosciences have dosed their first patients yet none can legally run longevity drug trials due to FDA regulatory constraints; Gary Marcus on Washington's arbitrary and potentially corrupt AI governance after the White House blocked Anthropic's Mythos model in less than 24 hours while benefiting OpenAI investors including Greg Brockman and Jared Kushner's brother — calling for an independent AI agency like Rep. Ro Khanna proposes; and the Trivium China Podcast on Xi Jinping's first foreign trip of 2026 to North Korea after nearly seven years, reasserting Chinese influence as Pyongyang tilts toward Russia with 16,000 soldiers fighting in Ukraine, plus the brewing EU-China trade war with Chinese EVs hitting 15% EU market share in April 2026.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup: OpenAI and Anthropic employees cash out $14B in shares; longevity startups NewLimit, Retro Biosciences, and Life Biosciences face FDA regulatory constraints preventing actual longevity drug trials; Gary Marcus on the White House's arbitrary, corrupt-appearing block of Anthropic's Mythos model and the case for an independent AI agency; and Xi Jinping's North Korea visit to reassert Chinese influence as Pyongyang tilts toward Russia, plus the EU-China trade war escalation with Chinese EVs at 15% EU market share.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - June 14, 2026
